topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam stared at the application form, a slow, deliberate movement of his finger tracing the line where he’d written his name. Across the desk, Maya was humming softly, reviewing her own meticulously crafted resume. A tight knot formed in Liam’s chest, a sensation hot and prickly. He reread the job description, a faint, unpleasant smile touching his lips. He knew Maya had been aiming for this role for months; she’d studied the company’s ethos, tailored her experience perfectly. He picked up his pen, the sound of the scratch against the paper sounding unnecessarily loud in the quiet office. He made a small, almost imperceptible alteration to a bullet point on his own document—a subtle enhancement of his own achievements. It was a tiny, private victory, a way to ensure that when the decision came, the contrast between them would be stark and undeniable.

[story 2]

"Well, look at that," Chloe drawled, leaning back in her chair with exaggerated nonchalance. She glanced over at Ben, whose jaw was visibly clenched. Chloe felt a surge of something sharp and unpleasant bubble up inside her, a need to see him stumble. She tapped her perfectly manicured nail against her monitor, a deliberate, rhythmic sound designed to irritate. Ben was focused entirely on the identical job posting, his brow furrowed in intense concentration. Chloe felt a strange, almost giddy sense of superiority watching him struggle to articulate his own qualifications. She decided to make her submission flawless, an absolute masterpiece, just to watch him watch it, and perhaps fail to measure up.

[story 3]

I watched Sarah pace by the water cooler, her movements jerky and overly energetic. She kept glancing over at Mark, who was calmly sipping his coffee, seemingly unbothered by the shared predicament. A distinct tightening began in my shoulders, a physical manifestation of the irritation churning in my gut. I reviewed my cover letter again, feeling a distinct urge to make every sentence sharper, more pointed. It wasn't about wanting the job; it was about ensuring that whatever outcome occurred, Sarah would see the clear difference in our approaches, the palpable gap between her earnest effort and my calculated precision. I smoothed down my shirt, a small, rigid gesture that spoke volumes about the internal tension I was trying to contain.
